A serious bug was found in production.
The issue was escalated to the backend team.
A patch was deployed within the hour.
The problem has been resolved.
Ana, thanks for the call. I heard the checkout was down this morning. How bad was it?
The impact was high. About fifteen percent of users could not pay, with high frequency during peak hours.
And what was the symptom? What did the users actually see?
A blank screen after they clicked Pay. It was reproducible, so the exact steps and a screenshot were attached.
Good. And what was done about it?
The issue was escalated to the backend team, the broken release was rolled back, and a patch was deployed. Downtime was under twenty minutes.
Perfect, that is exactly the level of detail I need. Is it fixed for good?
Yes. It was a regression from the last sprint. It has been resolved, and a new test was added so it cannot return.
A bug was found in production this morning.
The impact was high, with about fifteen percent of users affected.
The issue was escalated and a patch was deployed.
It was a regression, and it has been resolved.
The steps are reproducible, and a screenshot is attached.

Hi Ana, this is David from the product side. I just read your incident report about the checkout bug, and I want to say thank you. It was clear and short, exactly what we need for the stakeholders. Two quick things before I forward it up. First, please make the impact even more concrete at the top: how many users were affected, and during which hours. Second, I liked that you used the passive voice for the actions; keep it that way, because the leadership does not need the names, they need to know what was done. One more thing: add a single line at the end that says whether it has been fully resolved or whether we are still watching it. Great work, and let us keep reports this precise from now on.

Hi Ana, it is Priya, your team lead. I want to give you a quick tip on writing incident reports, because you are getting really good at them. The golden rule is simple: lead with the impact, not with the story. A stakeholder reads the first line and must already know how bad it is and who is affected. After that, be precise: give the symptom, the frequency, and whether the bug is reproducible. Then report the actions in the passive voice, so the report stays neutral and clean: the issue was escalated, the release was rolled back, a patch was deployed. And never be vague. Do not write 'some problems were seen'; write 'the payment call failed for fifteen percent of users during peak hours'. Concise and precise, always. That is how you build trust with the people who read your reports.

Here is how Ana wrote her incident report. First, she opened with the impact: about fifteen percent of users could not pay. Then she described the symptom, a blank screen after the user clicked Pay. Next, she reported what was done in the passive voice: the issue was escalated and a patch was deployed. After that, she explained the root cause, a regression from the last sprint. Finally, she confirmed that the problem had been resolved and a new test was added.
